sentences.text: OK, Dave Lux, D-A-V-E, not David, L-U-X.
Isidor Pinkasovich.
I-S-I-D-O-R and P-I-N-K-A-S-O-V-I-C-H.
Well, I have a Yiddish name, and Jewish, and Hebrew name.
That's the way it was in Europe-- [?
Srul ?] [?
Behr, ?] which is two names.
It seems like we all had two name, and that's Yiddish, and in Hebrew, it's [?
Ysrael ?] [?
Dove. ?]
April 12th, 1933.
62 Until next month.
Well, Czechoslovakia.
You want me to spell that?
It's kind of-- kind of long.
But the--
City, Negrovic, or Negrovitz-- I'm not sure how it's pronounced N-E-G-R-O-V-I-C.
Yeah, C with an accent on it.
OK.
OK, until I came to the United States, or-- or maybe shortly before that, I thought that was my real name, Pinkasovich.

And I discovered from relatives that-- that I found here in the United States-- actually, it started-- I met a-- I found a cousin in Israel-- that-- and through-- through my aunt here in the United States that my father's name was actually Lux, L-U-X.
So I always did want to-- to shorten my name.
And I was debating between Pinkas or whatever.
And that was a-- that was a natural choice.
And I-- that's what I did when I came to the United States.
I changed to what was my father's name, to Lux.
How long did you live in Czechoslovakia?
I left Czechoslovakia before the war right before my sixth birthday.
Yeah.
Starting when?
I remember my father was tall and thin.
And he was a baker.
And apparently, he always kind of kidded around a lot.
I have a son that has his kind of personality.
He's always clowning around.
And I remember my mother.
She was, I think, short and a little stocky.
I don't remember her ever clowning around.
She was more the serious type.
Maybe-- we probably had a pretty stressful type of life.
You know.
We were poor-- you know, that type of thing.
And we-- we lived-- the last place that we lived I remember was not our house.
It was-- there was a family more like in a farm environment that we were renting apparently.
And it was, from what I recollect, one room, big room, with a curtain that separate-- that divided it from the living quarters and the sleeping quarters.
There was no plumbing in the house.
We-- we had-- we used to bring in water from the outside.
And I recall in front of the house, there was a hand pump.
And I think we had an outhouse as far as a toilet.
And so that's what I remember about the house.
Yeah, I have a brother that lives in Jerusalem, a year older than me.
And we were always, like-- always together, inseparable, I guess.
And-- and people always used to ask us, are you twins?
Not that we looked alike.
But we always wore the same thing and were the same size more or less.
His name is-- is-- well, here we go with names.
Herman was his non-Jewish name.
And Yakov is his Hebrew name.
He kept his name Pinkasovich, because by the time that I changed mine, he'd already got married.
And-- and he just-- in Israel, he was pretty comfortable with that name, I guess.
And I think later on, he just said-- his kids were starting to grow up.
And they were all comfortable with the name.
So he didn't-- didn't bother.
My father's-- excuse me-- name was Mordechai In fact, I remember my mother calling him Mordca, which is short-- short for Mordechai, and my mother's name was Esther.
Yeah.
I have absolutely don't remember any town.
It's-- all I can remember is that there was fields around us.
And I remember occasionally I'd see gypsies, caravans going by on the hills at a distance, you know, with the horses and wagons.
And I always felt a little bit afraid of them.
I think the thing is just the fear of something that-- that you're not familiar with.
But that was a frequent thing and gypsy caravans going by.
I remember right before we left that location, my brother being a year older was taken to a school.
And I guess he was about to be enrolled in a school, which never happened, because, you know, we-- we left because of what happened later.
Well, let me-- before I abandon this-- this era--
I'll go to that in a minute.
Some of the things I remember about my family-- I remember going on trips with my mother a lot to visit family like uncles, aunts, maybe and grandparents.
I don't ever remember going with my dad, my father.
And apparently, he was a working-- you know, he had to work.
He worked as a baker.
And so that's why with all these trips, my brother and I went with our mother.
I remember grandparents that we used to visit-- also, more or less like farmland.
I remember a lot of horses.
I remember a lot of cornfields.
And for some reason, I remember pears-- you know, small pears.
There was like umpteen trees-- pear trees.
But what-- what really kind of sticks in my mind is the interior of that house that-- that I-- was-- had some significant-- it was different.
And so it has significance.
And I thought it was my imagination till I described it to some of the relatives that I found later on.
And they said, yeah, my description was pretty accurate.
These houses, of course, they didn't have central heat.
So they depended on coal, or wood, or whatever.
So my grandparents had an oven in the house that looked more like a bakers oven, I think-- you know, these-- these heavy iron front, and covered by stucco, you know, stucco.
And they used to cook and bake in their-- bread, whatever.
And then there was steps going up from-- from the ground level to above the oven.
And then there was a flat area above the oven.
And we used to sleep up there.
It was great because it was warm.
And, yeah, that's the way it was.
Train.
Train.
Yeah, all the travel was done by locomotive train.
Yeah.
Oh, talking about train-- there was another incident that I remember very clearly.
And we-- we once were coming-- returning from a trip.
And at the railroad station, we were held.
My mother, and-- and I, and my brother.
I didn't know what was going on.
See, I didn't speak no Czech.
In those days, all I spoke was Yiddish.
So a lot of things I didn't understand.
But I-- I saw-- I knew we were in trouble.
Later, I thought, did she lose her tickets?
I don't know what happened.
But we were in-- they held us at the-- in the exit.
Who was they?
The authorities.
I don't know, the-- the-- people from the railroad.
It wasn't police.
It was the railroad.
OK.
And another thing that I had-- hadn't mentioned earlier-- even though my brother was older than me, as a child, he was-- he was not a well child.
And I kind of felt protective of him.
So anyway, my wife-- I'm sorry.
My-- my mother needed to call my dad to straighten out this problem.
I don't know if it was coming with money or what.
I don't know what happened.
But-- so there was no phones in those days, not in that area.
Anyway, so I-- my mother sent me to get my dad.
Now, remember, I ran all the way, because I knew there was-- it was something very important.
And I brought my dad-- bailed us out.
So I don't know.
But I remember that.
Oh, yeah.
Oh, I ran all the way.
Yeah.
OK, that's just some of  the things I remember.
Oh, yeah, in fact, before they enrolled my brother to-- to school, we were going to like Jewish preschool.
They call that-- what do they call that?
Cheder, cheder, cheder.
They call that, yeah.
And, you know, it's usually-- usually-- it's always, it's rabbis that train your religious studies from a very young age.
And for some reason, I have-- it's almost funny, I remember big horse flies.
You know.
You know.
Maybe the-- you know, it wasn't  a very clean area.
I don't know.
But it was-- it's funny how things stick in your mind-- like god, these big guys.
[LAUGHS]
Oh, yeah.
We were very Orthodox. yeah.

In fact, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember one time-- like, it's traditional for Jewish people on-- on-- on Sabbath, they had these traditional dishes that they used to serve-- almost every Jewish family.
But everybody knows about the fish.
OK.
But other-- other than the Jewish type fish they serve-- usually Friday night, there was also two other dishes that were very common.
I'm sure every family-- I mean, every family used to make it.
One is cholent, and one is kugel.
Cholent is a-- is beef with potatoes and beans, maybe barley also.
I'm not quite sure what the ingredients are.
But-- and it's-- it's cooked and simmered over a long period.
And-- and then during the Sabbath, Orthodox people don't cook.
So  they just keep it on a very low fire.
The other one, kugel, is not like a-- it's not a noodle kugel.
The traditional kugel was made from potatoes.
And it's almost like-- well, it's a potato kugel-- thick.
And-- anyway, that was the side dish kind of.
And that was the most-- I think just about every Jewish family used to-- used to make that.
Anyway, the point I want to make is my father being a baker, to help out my mother, he'd make this stuff, because he had these huge ovens, you know, in the bakery.
And so Sabbath morning, we-- my brother and I would go with him to the bakery and carry it, because Orthodox people are not allowed to haul stuff.
[LAUGHS] I mean, that's real Orthodox.
You know.
So my brother and I-- there used to be a pot with two handles.
I'd hold one.
He'd hold the other.
We'd carry it.
I remember, in fact, one time we spilled the thing.
And I got burned.
I don't know.
Like I said, I don't remember who dropped it, but that was one-- one occasion, yeah.
Oh, very orthodox, yeah.
No, I'm sure there was.
But, you know, I don't remember everything.
[LAUGHS] Yeah.
Oh, yeah, yeah, I remember, like I said, we visited  these grandparents.
And I remember visiting an uncle that-- my brother acknowledges this.
He had a red beard, because we have some-- some relatives with a red beard.
In fact, when I was in the service, I had a mustache that was red, even though my hair is brown.
So I have a little bit of that in me too.
Anyway, we visited this uncle.
And Orthodox Jews have-- the meat to make kosher has to be slaughtered in a certain way by what they called shochet.
And he was-- that was what he used to do.
He used to slaughter.
And I remember, especially like chickens, no problem.
You know.
But they had a special set up for cows.
And it was like chains and stuff.
With a-- you know, they would haul them up.
You know, so yeah, I remember that.
Yeah, I remember.
The set up, he had like in a shed.
A couple of things I remember from-- from those trips also.
There was a river where the water would run kind of fast-- you know, move pretty quickly.
And we-- my brother remembers this too.
They used to be like rafts that the locals used to use.
And-- you know, you picture a raft.
It's logs where people sit down.
These rafts, they would stand up.
And there was poles hold-- I could always draw a picture of it.
You know, there were poles where they hold onto.
And they would ride these rafts standing up.
And I mentioned this to some of my older relatives.
And they would say, yep, yeah.
That's right.
Yeah, I guess.
And-- so--
No, oh, you're talking about the rabbi?
Yeah, there was quite a few.
You know, I don't remember how many, but it wasn't like private lessons.
It was--
Yeah.
Well, later on, I remember.
But just to keep things in the right sequence, you asked earlier when we lived in this-- this place-- this rented house.
The first time we felt threatened is. oh, one day, we were visited by uniformed soldiers, I think, or police.
I don't know.
I was told they were Hungarian later on.
They had bright-green-- pretty bright-green uniforms with hats with, unless I'm imagining things, like feathers on them.
And they came into our house kind of aggressively.
And they searched our house.
And I just remember my mother was home, because my father was always at work.
And I could see she was worried.
All I know is the next day, we packed everything up.
And the people who we rented from were kind enough to accommodate us.
They brought their wagon-- horse and wagon.
We loaded everything on.
And we were out of there the next day.
So that's when things started to happen.
My first recollection is we-- we went to-- see, I have no idea how long the trip was or how far it was.
That, I don't remember.
But we ended up at a place.
It was a camp, all tents.
And what's significant-- you know, things are significant to me that happened the first time.
I remember when I came to England later on, the first time I ate corn flakes, it was such a strange experience, because I never had corn flakes.
You know what  I mean?
So here at this camp, the first thing that even now I can remember was the smell of like knackwurst.
And they served us meals at this camp in-- in these-- what do they call it?
Like-- like-- like the army have these can-- these tins.
Tin plates.
These-- these mess kits, I think you call.
Yeah.
That's what they served.
I remember knackwurst.
You know, I'm sure there was other-- other foods.
But that was significant, because it was different.
And funny enough, I met there-- there were two guys, brothers, kids more more less our own age.
One was a little older.
In fact, I remember his name.
His last name was Kolker-- K-O-L-K-E-R.
In fact, we reunited with these kids later on in England and in Israel.
And I think they are still in Israel.
[LAUGHS] Anyway, that was where we-- we-- the trip took us, to this camp.
I don't know how long we were there.
Could have been a few days-- could have been a week.
I don't think it was that long, because I don't remember too many experiences from there.
From there, I don't know how we got there.
The next thing I remember, we were in this huge building.
And we were with other families, multi-story.
And it was in a confined area with Slovakian military of something with guards.
I mean, we weren't treated badly.
But we weren't allowed to leave.
OK?
And I think they called them Hlinka Guards.
Later on, I thought what the heck is Hlinka Guards?
Because that's German.
[GERMAN] means left.
Now, it couldn't be-- and yet they were like-- I heard they weren't too good to the Jewish people, these Hlinka Guards.
But [GERMAN] means left.
And Nazis  are right.
So I don't have an answer for that.
[LAUGHS] But anyway, that was what happened next.
We were in that building.
And you were asking about do I remember my father what he did as far as clowning around?
Yeah, I remember spending a Purim there and my father getting dressed up, you know, of putting makeup on his face and clowning around.
So I remember spending Purim there.
Were there other relatives there with you?
No, just-- just us-- just my family, our family.
And let's see, there isn't a whole I remember from that place, because we were confined.
Nothing.
They didn't tell us a thing.
I remember one time we went on a field trip-- you know, kids.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In fact, that area I was-- if I recall was called Rote Brucke, which is, in German, Red Bridge.
So that was the name of that-- Rote Brucke, yeah, something like that.
I guess Slovakia being close to Germany, they used a lot of German.
You know.
It was people from the-- I mean, even though we were in a restricted-- and-- you know, were confined and had limited movement-- I'm sure with passes and stuff, they allowed some freedom.
I mean, it wasn't like-- it wasn't a concentration camp or anything like that.
And as far as I recall, we were not treated badly at that point.
Anyway, it was while we were there.
I don't know how long.
I remember this-- this is when the process started of us going to England, I guess.
I remember this young, blonde lady, girl, talking to my parents.
I haven't the faintest idea what they were talking about.
I remember her talking to some other families.
What it turned out to be is apparently she was trying to talk him into let-- letting my parents send us to England because there was a war coming or something like that.
And I think the lady came back twice, at least.
The second time she must have convinced my parents.
And let's see--
No, later on, I thought how come I have a blank as far as that is concerned.
Then I-- I guess just logically in order to communicate with my parents, she had to talk either German, Czech, or Hungarian.
If she spoke German, I might have understood some because I spoke Yiddish.
OK.
My parents didn't speak German.
But they spoke Yiddish.
So I-- she must have been able to speak, in addition being English, either Czech or Hungarian.
She was English?
Oh, yeah, she was sent from England, yeah.
That I knew.
For some reason, I knew she was English-- blonde, English girl, yeah.
Anyway, I  remember the-- the-- right before we left our parents, my mother-- my father was always in the background from some reason.
She was really crying.
And my brother and I guess couldn't understand what all the fuss was about.
She wanted to know if we wanted to go.
And we didn't understand what it was all about.
We thought-- we thought go where?
For a trip?
Why-- why is she crying?
You know?
We didn't understand.
She was asking us if we really wanted to go away.
And that's-- so, you know, were a little bit annoyed, well, not annoyed, just totally couldn't understand why she was upset-- crying.
You know.
Well, that's what it was.
You know.
And-- and to this day, I don't understand-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I get a little emotional at times.
It's hard for me to understand how-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
My parents-- they-- they were not city people.
They didn't really know what was going on in the world.
We lived out in the sticks.
Let's face it.
And for someone who-- who would have been informed, they would have recognized and understood the threat.
And it seemed like they would be the first to say, here, take them.
You know, it's like some-- some parents threw babies out of trains, because they knew they're going to get killed.
So they-- they were realistic.
But-- but my parents-- they didn't know what was going on.
And yet, that this young English girl was able to convince them to let go of kids.
[CRYING] And that's really weird.
That's really strange.
But I guess that's why I'm alive.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah.
Shortly after that, I'll tell you all about-- I remember the trip.
I mean, it's like clear in my mind.
We will picked up in a black car.
In those days, they were all black cars, I guess.
You know.
And there was this English lady that came with us.
And there was one little girl.
I haven't the faintest idea who she is-- about our age, maybe a year older, and my brother.
And that was all the passengers in that car.
And we-- we drove off to Prague.
OK, yeah, I'm sorry.
I got--
